Q1: I tried to keep things simple because I don't have a lot of time or energy to spare. I usually studied in the evenings after everything else was done, mostly listening to lecture recordings while doing other tasks around the house. With nine online courses, I couldn't dedicate tons of time to each one, so I'd rotate through them based on what felt most urgent. My study routine was more about survival than perfection, honestly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, maybe studying a bit each day, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Being a steady performer doesn't mean I had a steady schedule—it means I figured out how to make it work even when things got chaotic. Deadlines were really what drove my timing more than anything else.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting a notification about new content. I logged in pretty frequently—about 30 times throughout the course—mostly to check what was due or to access the video lectures. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was on top of things, even if I didn't do much once I was there. The LMS was like my home base for knowing what I needed to tackle next.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS every day or two. I'd wake up, check my phone, and eventually end up on the platform just to see if anything new was posted. The discussion boards also pulled me back because I tried to stay involved there. It became part of my daily rhythm, even if I wasn't always actively studying when I logged in.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I watched over 170 minutes of video content, and I'd often replay sections while taking notes or just absorbing the information. The discussion posts were also valuable because hearing other perspectives helped concepts click for me. Audio-based content just works better with how my brain processes things.
Q6: I tended to ignore the reading materials and text-heavy resources because they just don't stick for me the same way. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what actually helped me learn, and reading dense PDFs wasn't it. I also minimally engaged with peer interactions—I know my count shows zero, but I just didn't have the energy for that kind of back-and-forth. It felt like an extra burden I couldn't take on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on exams or assignments. I also valued anything I could listen to rather than read, since that matches how I learn best. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my grade, I'd skip it. With so many courses and limited hours, I had to be strategic about where I put my effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff always fell to the bottom of my list. There's a different weight to something when you know it counts toward your grade versus when it's just 'recommended.' I wish I had more time for the optional things, but realistically, I had to focus on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them offline, especially when I didn't have reliable internet or was doing chores. I'd also make voice memos summarizing what I learned and play them back to myself later. A lot of my studying happened through headphones while I was cooking or walking—none of that would show up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but completely offline. I'd download lectures at the library, then study at home where my internet is spotty. I also spent time talking through concepts out loud to myself, which is how I process information. The LMS would just show I accessed something once, but I might have listened to it five or six times offline.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue studying them offline for days. With low resources and unreliable internet, it made more sense to grab everything I needed in one session and work with it later. My actual engagement with the content was way higher than my access logs would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when I was feeling sad and overwhelmed. The stress would build up and my motivation would tank—I'd know I should be studying but couldn't bring myself to care. The mindfulness exercises they offered actually helped me push through those periods. But there were stretches where engaging felt like moving through thick fog.
Q13: The workload from having nine courses was honestly brutal sometimes. Fatigue was a constant companion, and I'd often have to choose which course got my attention on any given day. Competing responsibilities outside of school also ate into my study time. There were days I'd log into the LMS, see everything I needed to do, and just feel paralyzed by it all.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be logged in, technically watching content, but not absorbing anything because I was too tired or stressed. Those sessions felt hollow—like I was going through the motions without actually learning. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but the benefit wasn't there.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Part of it was the sadness I've been dealing with—it saps your motivation even when you logically know you should be working. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same feeling. The mindfulness practice helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still hard.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd probably drift aimlessly through the material. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading everything I needed and cramming. It's not the healthiest approach, but with low motivation and high stress, external pressure was often the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got good feedback, it gave me a boost and made me want to put more effort into the next assignment. But when feedback was slow or vague, I'd feel disconnected from the course. The 17-hour average response time on my end partly reflects waiting to see how I did before diving into new material.
Q18: The course structure helped because it gave me clear milestones to work toward. Having organized modules meant I could see what was coming and plan around my limited study time. But the structure also meant everything felt rigid—if I fell behind, catching up felt overwhelming. The discussion requirements kept me somewhat accountable, which I needed.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. But the heavy reading requirements made things harder—I'd spend so much time trying to get through text when I could have learned the same thing faster through audio. Also, the lack of peer interaction opportunities that felt natural made me feel isolated sometimes, even though I was technically in a class with others.
Q20: I think students might be searching for something—maybe they're confused about what's actually required, or they're trying to find resources that match how they learn. High activity could also mean someone is struggling and keeps coming back hoping things will click. It doesn't necessarily mean they're slacking; they might just be lost or overwhelmed.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My attendance was really low and I only completed about half my assignments, but I still got a Merit grade with decent exam scores. I studied a lot offline, so my actual engagement was much higher than my LMS activity showed. The system can't see you listening to downloaded lectures on repeat while doing dishes.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you access, how long you're on the platform. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see if you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They definitely can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether the material is actually sinking in. It's like measuring the container without knowing what's inside.
Q23: Being engaged means the material is actually getting into my head and making sense. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I'm on the platform—it's about whether I'm thinking about the concepts, connecting them to other things I know, and feeling like I'm growing. True engagement has an emotional component too; it's caring about what you're learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the emotional journey I went through—the sadness, the stress, the moments when mindfulness helped me get back on track. It wouldn't capture all the audio studying I did offline or the voice memos I made for myself. It also wouldn't show the resilience it took to keep going with limited resources and low motivation. My Merit grade came from a lot of invisible effort.
